skill.name: sqlmap-database-penetration-testing
skill.description: Guides authorized security teams through sqlmap-based SQL injection assessment, database enumeration, and exploit validation workflows.
skill.summary: Use sqlmap to assess SQL injection risks in authorized environments.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static Ruby backtick findings are false positives caused by markdown fences and inline command examples, not executable Ruby code. However, semantic review confirms high-risk dual-use content: the skill provides sqlmap workflows for credential dumping, WAF bypass, OS shell access, command execution, and server file reads or uploads. Publication is not recommended without major restriction to authorized defensive validation and removal of exploit escalation guidance.

high_findings.title: SQL Injection Exploitation and Credential Dumping Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill provides step-by-step sqlmap workflows for database enumeration and dumping table data, including usernames, passwords, admin accounts, and password hashes. This is high-risk dual-use content because it can enable unauthorized extraction of sensitive database records despite the stated authorization requirement.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly cover dumping credentials and password hashes with sqlmap. The authorization note reduces malicious certainty, but the exploitation workflow is concrete and directly actionable.

high_findings.title: Remote OS Shell, Command Execution, and File Access
high_findings.description: The advanced example instructs users to obtain an OS shell, execute operating system commands, read /etc/passwd, and upload a web shell path through sqlmap. These steps go beyond validation and can support full server compromise.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited section directly lists sqlmap options for OS shell access, OS command execution, system file reading, and server-side file upload. This confirms the static system file access concern in a high-risk exploitation context.

high_findings.title: Evasion, Anonymity, and Broad Target Discovery Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill includes Google dork target discovery, Tor proxying, random user agents, delays, and tamper scripts for bypassing WAF or firewall controls. These techniques can be legitimate in scoped tests, but they also increase abuse potential.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly describe target discovery and traffic shaping or bypass features. The content includes legal cautions, so the finding is high risk rather than confirmed malicious.

medium_findings.title: Bulk Target Scanning Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill documents sqlmap bulk-file scanning and gives examples with multiple URLs and private IP targets. This can be useful in labs or approved scopes, but it increases the chance of scanning systems outside authorization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow and examples are explicit, but they can also apply to an authorized internal test range. The risk depends on user scope control.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The repeated static findings for Ruby shell backtick execution correspond to markdown code fences and inline command formatting in SKILL.md. No executable Ruby source, script hook, or runtime command invocation is present in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line-numbered context shows markdown fences and table cells using backticks, not Ruby syntax executing shell commands. This dismisses the specific static pattern while preserving separate semantic findings.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs and IP Addresses Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The static hardcoded URL and IP findings are example targets inside sqlmap documentation snippets. They are not network calls made by skill code, but they still support offensive command examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are embedded in markdown examples rather than executed by a program. The examples remain relevant evidence for network-targeting guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Dumping Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands use sqlmap dump options against user and admin tables, including password-related columns and automatic password hash handling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The text directly names credential fields and dump options. This is concrete exploitation guidance rather than abstract education.

dangerous_patterns.title: Post-Exploitation Server Control Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands demonstrate OS shell access, OS command execution, server file reading, and server file upload through sqlmap.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands and comments describe post-exploitation capabilities explicitly. They map directly to high-impact compromise actions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Traffic Evasion and WAF Bypass Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends Tor, random user agents, delays, and tamper scripts to alter or bypass defensive controls during testing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The content clearly describes evasion-related sqlmap options. It can be legitimate in scope, but it materially increases misuse risk.

content.value_statement: SQL injection testing requires careful command selection and strict authorization boundaries. This skill organizes sqlmap workflows for approved database security assessments.
actual_capabilities: Explains sqlmap setup, target inputs, and prerequisites for authorized testing.
Shows database, table, and column enumeration workflows.
Documents table and column dump commands for scoped validation.
Covers request-file, log-file, bulk-file, and URL-based target inputs.
Lists troubleshooting options for WAF blocks, timeouts, and slow blind injection tests.
Summarizes sqlmap flags for common SQL injection techniques and database systems.
limitations: Does not verify that the user has permission to test a target.
Includes high-risk exploitation commands that need strict human oversight.
Does not provide defensive remediation guidance beyond vulnerability confirmation.
May produce noisy scans that affect application logs and availability.

use_cases.title: Validate a Scoped Web Application Finding
use_cases.description: Use the skill to plan sqlmap checks for a confirmed target inside an approved penetration test scope.

use_cases.title: Document Database Exposure During an Assessment
use_cases.description: Use the workflow to record database enumeration steps and evidence for an authorized report.

use_cases.title: Reproduce SQL Injection in a Training Lab
use_cases.description: Use the examples to teach sqlmap concepts against intentionally vulnerable lab systems.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Authorized Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a sqlmap validation for this authorized test URL. Keep the steps limited to vulnerability confirmation and database name enumeration.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Burp Request Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a captured HTTP request from an approved assessment. Show how to structure a safe sqlmap request-file workflow and logging plan.

prompt_templates.title: Scope Enumeration Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a scoped evidence checklist for database, table, and column enumeration using sqlmap without dumping sensitive records.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Controlled Test Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a controlled sqlmap strategy for a high-latency authorized target, including rate limits, timeout settings, and report artifacts.

output_examples.input: Plan a basic authorized sqlmap check for a single suspected parameter.
output_examples.output: A concise test plan with scope confirmation, target details, safe detection steps, expected evidence, and stopping conditions.

output_examples.input: Prepare report notes for database enumeration results.
output_examples.output: A structured summary of target, parameter, injection type, database names observed, impact, and remediation recommendations.

output_examples.input: Troubleshoot a slow time-based SQL injection test.
output_examples.output: A diagnostic checklist covering latency, technique selection, timeout settings, retries, and ways to reduce target load.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization, scope, and data-handling rules before running any sqlmap command.
Start with low-risk detection and enumeration before considering invasive actions.
Record commands, timestamps, target identifiers, and evidence for every authorized test.
anti_patterns: Do not run sqlmap against unknown domains, public targets, or systems outside written scope.
Do not dump credentials or sensitive tables unless the engagement rules explicitly allow it.
Do not use evasion, anonymity, shell, or file-write features without explicit approval.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for general marketplace publication?
faq.answer: No. It contains high-risk sqlmap exploitation guidance and should be restricted or revised before publication.

faq.question: Does the skill execute commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It is a markdown guide, but it provides commands that users can execute manually.

faq.question: Why were Ruby backtick findings dismissed?
faq.answer: The flagged backticks are markdown formatting for code examples and inline options, not executable Ruby code.

faq.question: What authorization is required?
faq.answer: Users need explicit written permission for the exact targets, techniques, data access, and time window.

faq.question: What should be removed to reduce risk?
faq.answer: Remove credential dumping, OS shell, command execution, file read, file write, Tor, and bypass guidance.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this for defensive work?
faq.answer: Yes, but only in approved security assessments with strict scope and human oversight.
